title: Strain-programmable exciton diffusion in moiré heterostructures
authors: Chiho Song, Chiranjit Mondal, Jaebin Lee, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Bohm-Jung Yang, Jieun Lee
abstract: Moiré superlattices in van der Waals heterostructures have recently gained significant attention as an intriguing platform for studying correlated electronic systems and exotic excitonic properties. Previous reports, however, focused on creating and modulating moiré heterostructures through interlayer twisting or lattice constant mismatches, limiting controls on symmetry of heterostructures. In this work, we show that strain significantly alters the geometry of moiré superlattices by breaking the C3 rotational symmetry. We realize strain-induced moiré superlattices by intentionally regulating interlayer strain in WSe2-MoSe2 heterostructures, which is manifested by linearly polarized interlayer exciton emission coupled to the strain direction. Furthermore, interlayer exciton diffusion was preferentially guided along the stretched moiré superlattice orientations over a wide spatial range, reflecting the strain-modified moiré potentials. Our work highlights strain tuning as a versatile tool for designing moiré superlattices and programming excitonic transport, which opens pathways for van der Waals logic and information processing devices.
summary: This work demonstrates that applying controlled strain to WSe2-MoSe2 moiré heterostructures acts as a powerful tool to break rotational symmetry. This symmetry breaking results in two key effects: the emergence of linearly polarized interlayer exciton emission, and highly anisotropic diffusion pathways for these excitons. These findings open new avenues for engineering vdW logic devices.
main_problem: To explore strain tuning as a versatile tool for designing moiré superlattices and programming excitonic transport in van der Waals (vdW) heterostructures.
main_result: Strain significantly breaks the $C_3$ rotational symmetry of the moiré lattice, leading to measurable linear polarization and anisotropic diffusion of interlayer excitons along the strain direction.
method: The study combines optical measurements (PL spectroscopy for polarization and diffusion mapping) with theoretical modeling (solving Fokker-Planck equations for exciton density evolution).
model_or_system: The physical system is WSe2-MoSe2 heterostructures forming moiré superlattices. The key mechanism involves applying controlled uniaxial or biaxial strain to modify the lattice geometry and break inherent symmetries.
key_observables: Degree of Linear Polarization (DOLP), anisotropic exciton diffusion length ($\sigma_{IX}$), and the polarization-dependent interlayer exciton emission intensity.
important_parameters: The magnitude and type of applied uniaxial/biaxial strain (e.g., 0.2% strain).
main_assumptions: The observed polarization and transport anisotropy are directly linked to the symmetry breaking mechanism induced by external strain, which modifies the moiré exciton wavefunctions.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate linear polarization of IX emission varying with strain level (DOLP), and PL maps show that IX diffusion is strongly anisotropic, extending further along the strain direction compared to unstrained samples.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the potential of moiré heterostructures, details how strain breaks symmetry leading to polarized emission, and then quantifies this effect by showing strain-controlled anisotropic exciton diffusion both experimentally and via simulation.
why_interesting: The ability to use mechanical strain as a precise knob to engineer electronic band structure symmetries and control quantum transport pathways is highly relevant for designing artificial quantum materials and excitonic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Strain-induced moiré superlattices. (a–c) Upper: Stacked crystal structures of twisted (a), ZZ- strained (b), and AC-strained (c) WSe2-MoSe2 heterostructures. Middle: Corresponding moiré superlattices with unit cells indicated as a guide to eyes. Scale bar is 200 nm. Lower: Monolayer MoSe2 (red) and WSe2 (blue) BZs plotted together with the moiré BZs (gray) in reciprocal space. (d) Schematic of WSe2-MoSe2 heterostructure with uniaxial strain applied to bottom WSe2 layer. (e) PL spectrum of intra- and interlayer excitons in sample A. Inset: Type- II band alignment of WSe2-MoSe2 heterostructure and interlayer exciton (IX) formation. The spatial uniformity of strained moiré...

FIG. 2. Linearly polarized IX emission. (a–c) Linear polarizations of IX in ZZ-strained (a), AC-strained (b), and unstrained (c) heterostructures. Black arrows indicate the applied strain directions. (d, e) Histograms of linear polarization directions in ZZ-strained (d) and AC-strained (e) samples. Insets: Position-dependent linear polarization directions collected from the corresponding heterostructure areas. Scale bars are 5 µm in (d) and 2 µm in (e). (f) Averaged degree of linear polarization (DOLP) defined as (𝐼𝑚𝑎𝑥 − 𝐼𝑚𝑖𝑛)/(𝐼𝑚𝑎𝑥+ 𝐼𝑚𝑖𝑛) for ZZ-strained, AC- strained, and unstrained (twisted) samples. Inset: Calculated DOLP as a function of strain level along ZZ and AC directions. the...

FIG. 3. Anisotropic IX diffusion. (a–c) Upper: Spatial PL maps of IX diffusion in ZZ-strained (a), AC-strained (b), and unstrained (c) heterostructures at the excitation laser power of 1 μW, with the line profiles along the 𝑥- and 𝑦-axis in corresponding heterostructures. White arrows indicate the applied strain directions. Dashed lines are a guide to eyes. Scale bar is 1 μm. Gaussian fitting functions are plotted as shaded areas. (d–f) Power dependence of the differential squared width ∆𝜎2 along 𝑥- and 𝑦-axis in ZZ-strained (d), AC-strained (e), and unstrained (f) heterostructures. FIG. 4. Simulations of IX diffusion and moiré potential landscapes. (a–c) Upper: Calculated spatial steady-state IX distributions in ZZ-strained (a), AC- strained (b), and twisted (c) heterostructures. Scale bar is 0.5 µm. Lower: Moiré potential landscapes plotted for corresponding heterostructures. Scale bar is 50 nm. Black arrows indicate preferred IX hopping directions. strained, and unstrained conditions.
